title: Path density plots
text: Open R install load necessary packages. can install packages running following code: Next, install {thermoclustr} package running following code: Define path Hefty output (.txt file). example: aware R uses forward-slashes (/) separate folders. Next, import .txt file R using function read_hefty():

title: Path density plots
text: plot density paths, simply use function plot_path_density():  uses package’s default values smoothing binning creates ggplot type graphic. can customize smoothing density binning changing parameters - bins - number filled contours. GOF_rank - Selects n highest GOF ranked paths. densify - extra points added along individual paths avoid vertices path evaluated? n - many equally-spaced extra points added along vertices path (densify=TRUE). samples - Size random subsample paths reduce computation time.  Finally, can customize ggplot, axes labels, change colors, reverse axes:
code: # set `theme_classic()` as the default ggplot theme theme_set(theme_classic())  plot1 <- plot_path_density_filled(tT_paths, show.legend = FALSE) print(plot1) plot2 <- plot_path_density_filled(tT_paths, bins = 25, GOF_rank = 5, densify = TRUE, n = 100, max_distance = 1, samples = 100, show.legend = FALSE) print(plot2) plot2 +   labs(     title = "Kernel density of t-T paths",     caption = "data from Fontes Pinto et al. (in prep.)",     x = "Time (Ma)",     y = bquote("Temperature (" * degree * "C)")   ) +   coord_cartesian(expand = FALSE) +   scale_x_continuous(transform = "reverse", position = "top") +   scale_y_continuous(transform = "reverse") +   guides(fill = "none")

title: Path clustering
text: First, calculate dissimilarities paths using Hausdorff distance: alternative measure dissimilarities Fréchet distance using dist = 'Frechet'.

title: Path clustering
text: Whether data actually clustered can checked using Hopkins statistic (H), .e. mean nearest neighbor distance random data set divided sum mean nearest neighbor distances real across simulated data set interpret Hopkins statistics? data D uniformly distributed, distances reals simulated data close , thus H 0.5. However, clusters present D, distances artificial points substantially larger real ones expectation, thus value H increase (Han, Kamber, Pei 2012). value H higher 0.75 indicates clustering tendency 90% confidence level. null alternative hypotheses defined follow: Null hypothesis: data set D uniformly distributed (.e., meaningful clusters) Alternative hypothesis: data set D uniformly distributed (.e., contains meaningful clusters) can conduct Hopkins Statistic test iteratively, using 0.5 threshold reject alternative hypothesis. , H < 0.5, unlikely D statistically significant clusters. Put words, value Hopkins statistic close 1, can reject null hypothesis conclude dataset D significantly clusterable data. result shows data likely clusterable.

title: Path clustering
text: optimal number clusters can estimated using path_nbclust() based average silhouette width:  method recommends 3 clusters t-T paths.

title: Path clustering
text: Finally cluster data using estimate optimal number clusters. clustering done using cluster_paths(), returns data.frame containing path number assigned cluster: can quickly check many paths assigned cluster: example, paths almost equally distributed two clusters. can now merge cluster result original path data.frame: can calculate also path statistics (e.g. median paths, 5% 95% quantiles) cluster: Plot cluster modeled t-T paths:  final look distribution paths:
code: cluster_res <- paths_filtered |>   cluster_paths(k = 3, method = "hclust")  # print the first rows of the cluster result: head(cluster_res) #>   segment cluster #> 1       1       1 #> 2      10       2 #> 3     100       1 #> 4     101       2 #> 5     102       1 #> 6     103       1 count_cluster(cluster_res) #>   1   2   3  #> 152  41 131 path_families <- right_join(   cluster_res, paths_filtered$paths,   join_by(segment) ) path_families_binned <- path_families |>   dplyr::group_by(cluster) |>   densify_cluster() |>   path_statistics()  head(path_families_binned) #> # A tibble: 6 × 13 #>   cluster bins     time_min time_median time_max temp_mean temp_sd temp_IQR #>   <fct>   <fct>       <dbl>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 1       (-0.4,8]     0           3.14     8.00      32.1    8.87     11.3 #> 2 1       (8,16]       8.00       11.9     16.0       38.6   10.8      16.4 #> 3 1       (16,24]     16.0        20.0     24.0       42.1   11.3      17.1 #> 4 1       (24,32]     24.0        27.9     32.0       46.4   10.8      15.6 #> 5 1       (32,40]     32.0        36.2     40.0       51.1   10.4      13.2 #> 6 1       (40,48]     40.0        43.8     48.0       56.4   10.2      12.2 #> # ℹ 5 more variables: temp_median <dbl>, temp_max <dbl>, temp_min <dbl>, #> #   temp_5 <dbl>, temp_95 <dbl> ggplot(data = path_families_binned) +   geom_ribbon(     aes(x = time_median, ymin = temp_5, ymax = temp_95, group = cluster, fill = cluster),     stat = "stepribbon",     inherit.aes = FALSE,     alpha = .66   ) +   geom_step(     aes(x = time_median, y = temp_median, color = cluster),     lwd = 1.5   ) +   scale_color_viridis_d(aesthetics = c("fill", "color"),                         begin = .1, end = .9) +    # Axes settings:   scale_y_reverse() +   scale_x_reverse(position = "top") +    # Labels:   labs(     title = "Example",     subtitle = "Path families",     x = "Time (Ma)",     y = bquote("Temperature (" * degree * "C)")   ) +   # facet_wrap(vars(cluster)) + # uncomment if you want each cluster in a separate plot   coord_cartesian(expand = FALSE, ylim = c(max_temperature, 0), xlim = c(max_time, 0)) path_families |>   dplyr::select(segment, cluster, Comp_GOF) |>   dplyr::distinct() |>    ggplot(aes(Comp_GOF, fill = cluster)) +   geom_histogram(color = "white", binwidth = .025) +   scale_color_viridis_d(aesthetics = c("fill", "color"), begin = .1, end = .9) +   coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 1), expand = FALSE, clip = "off") +   labs(     x = "goodness-of-fit statistic", title = "Histogram of path's goodness-of-fit values"   )

title: Path clustering
text: Another way look path-dissimilarities transforming Hausdorff distance matrix multidimensional scaling (MDS). MDS reduces dimensions original dataset, paths plotted points. Thereby, plot “maps” dissimilarities way similar paths plot close points, different paths far apart. path_diss() function already calculated MDS coordinates, can now plotted colored cluster results. > Note MDS dimension-reducing transformation Thus, 2D representation modified projection true relationship data thus can depict distortions overlaps. visualization offers explore correlations path similarities (clusters) properties paths, goodness--fit values Hefty model:  default, classical metric MDS used. Alternatively, methods Kruskal’s non-metric MDS, Sammon’s non-metric MDS, Uniform Manifold Approximation Projection (UMAP), t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) can used:
code: tT_diss_mds <- as.data.frame(tT_diss$mds) |>    bind_cols(cluster_res)  ggplot(tT_diss_mds, aes(V1, V2, color = cluster)) +   geom_point() +   scale_color_viridis_d(aesthetics = c("fill", "color"), begin = .1, end = .9) +   labs(title = "Multidimensional scaling of paths", x = "Dim 1", y = "Dim 2") # join the mds coordinates with the path properties dplyr::left_join(   tT_diss_mds,    dplyr::distinct(dplyr::select(paths, segment, Comp_GOF)),    dplyr::join_by(segment)   ) |>  ggplot(aes(V1, V2, color = Comp_GOF, shape = cluster)) +   geom_point() +   scale_color_viridis_b(     "Goodnes-of-fit (GOF)",     limits = c(0, 1),     breaks = seq(0, 1, .1),     oob = squish   ) +   labs(title = "Multidimensional scaling of paths", x = "Dim 1", y = "Dim 2") # Classical MDS tT_cmds <- stats::cmdscale(tT_diss$diss)  tT_cmds_coords <- data.frame(tT_cmds) |>   dplyr::bind_cols(cluster_res) |>    dplyr::mutate(method = 'cMDS')   # Kruskal's non-metric MDS (nMDS) tT_nmds <- MASS::isoMDS(tT_diss$diss) #> initial  value 24.573803  #> final  value 24.573021  #> converged tT_nmds_coords <- data.frame(tT_nmds$points) |>    dplyr::bind_cols(cluster_res) |>    dplyr::mutate(method = "Kruskal's nMDS")   # Sammon's nMDS tT_sammon <- MASS::sammon(tT_diss$diss) #> Initial stress        : 0.10203 #> stress after   3 iters: 0.08971 tT_sammon_coords <- data.frame(tT_sammon$points) |>    dplyr::bind_cols(cluster_res) |>    dplyr::mutate(method = "Sammon's nMDS")   # t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) tT_tsne <- Rtsne::Rtsne(tT_diss$diss, is_distance = TRUE) tT_tsne_coords <- data.frame(tT_tsne$Y) |>    dplyr::bind_cols(cluster_res) |>    dplyr::mutate(method = "t-SNE")   # Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) if(require(RcppHNSW)) library(RcppHNSW) tT_umap <- uwot::umap2(tT_diss$diss) tT_umap_coords <- data.frame(tT_umap) |>   dplyr::bind_cols(cluster_res) |>    dplyr::mutate(method = 'UMAP')  # Combine and plot: dplyr::bind_rows(   tT_cmds_coords,   tT_nmds_coords,   tT_sammon_coords,   tT_tsne_coords,   tT_umap_coords ) |>    ggplot(aes(X1, X2, color = cluster)) +   geom_point() +   facet_wrap(vars(method), scales = 'free') +   scale_color_viridis_d() +   labs(x = "Dim 1", y = "Dim 2")

title: Authors and Citation
text: Tobias Stephan. Author, maintainer. Taís Fontes Pinto. Author, copyright holder.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Stephan T, Fontes Pinto T (2026). thermoclustr: Analyzing Inverse Thermal History Models. R package version 0.0.0.9011, https://tobiste.github.io/thermoclustr/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {thermoclustr: Analyzing Inverse Thermal History Models},   author = {Tobias Stephan and Taís {Fontes Pinto}},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9011},   url = {https://tobiste.github.io/thermoclustr/}, }

title: Analyzing Inverse Thermal History Models
text: goal {thermoclustr} provide tools analyze thermal history models thermochronology, including estimating cooling path densities, path families.

title: Analyzing Inverse Thermal History Models
text: can install development version {thermoclustr} GitHub :

title: Analyzing Inverse Thermal History Models
text: following minimal example shows import data, visualize paths path density well filter, cluster t-T paths 3 path families. HeFTy model contains modeled paths, initial model constraints, weighted mean path, summary statistics mineral grains. {thermochlustr} imports model list contains aforementioned features list entries. Thus, visualize paths need extract path object imported model tT_paths:  path density can visualized using plot_path_density_filled():  cluster data, following steps required: Finally, visualization clustered t-T paths:
code: library(thermoclustr) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)  # load example dataset of a HeFTy model output path2myfile <- system.file("112-73_30_H1_50-inv.txt", package = "thermoclustr") tT_paths <- read_hefty(path2myfile)  # set `theme_classic()` as the default ggplot theme theme_set(theme_classic()) # (Optional) Create a subset of interest of the data: tT_paths_cropped <- crop_paths(tT_paths, time = c(0, 400), temperature = c(0, 250))  tT_paths_cropped$paths |>   ggplot(aes(time, temperature, color = Comp_GOF, group = segment)) +   geom_path() +   scale_color_viridis_c("GOF") +   scale_x_reverse(position = "top") +   scale_y_reverse() plot_path_density_filled(tT_paths_cropped, geom = "raster") +   scale_x_reverse(position = "top") +   scale_y_reverse() +   scale_fill_viridis_c() # Cluster the paths paths_cluster <- cluster_paths(tT_paths_cropped, k = 3)  # Join with path dataset paths_clustered <- merge(   tT_paths_cropped$paths,   paths_cluster,   by = "segment" ) paths_clustered |>   ggplot(     aes(       x = time,       y = temperature,       color = cluster,       group = segment     )   ) +   geom_path() +   scale_x_reverse(position = "top") +   scale_y_reverse() +   scale_color_viridis_d("Cluster", begin = .1, end = .9)

title: Remove orphan paths — clean_HeFTy
text: Sometimes HeFTy produces paths start time = 0. "orphan" paths valid removed. function removes paths exist times greater min_time (default = 0).

title: Remove orphan paths — clean_HeFTy
text: x data.frame time, temperature segment columns min_time numeric. Minimum time consider path valid (default 0). Paths segments exist times greater min_time removed.

title: Remove orphan paths — clean_HeFTy
text: data.frame orphan paths removed.

title: Clustering thermal histories models — cluster_paths
text: Groups t-T paths "path families" based Hausdorff Fréchet distance paths.

title: Clustering thermal histories models — cluster_paths
code: cluster_paths(   x,   k,   dist = c("Hausdorff", "Frechet"),   method = c("hclust", "kmeans", "pam", "dbscan", "hdbscan", "specc", "diana", "agnes",     "clara", "fanny"),   naming = c("asis", "GOF", "size"),   warn = TRUE,   threshold = 0.01,   ... )

title: Clustering thermal histories models — cluster_paths
text: x either object class "HeFTy" (output read_hefty()), object "tTdiss" (output path_diss()), data.frame containing time, temperature, segment columns modeled paths. k integer scalar vector desired number groups. Ignored dist equal dbscan hdbscan (see Note). dist character. Algorithm calculate dissimilarity matrix (distance) lines; either Hausdorff (default), Frechet. method character. Clustering method applied. Currently implemented "hclust" Hierarchical Clustering using stats::hclust(), default) "kmeans" K-Means Clustering using stats::kmeans()) "pam" Partitioning Around Medoids using cluster::pam() "dbscan" Density-based Spatial Clustering Applications Noise (DBSCAN) using dbscan::dbscan() (see Note) "hdbscan" Hierarchical DBSCAN using dbscan::hdbscan() (see Note) "specc" Spectral Clustering using kernlab::specc() "agnes" Agglomerative hierarchical clustering using cluster::agnes() "diana" Divisive hierarchical clustering using cluster::diana() "clara" Clustering Large Applications using cluster::clara() "fanny" Fuzzy Analysis Clustering using cluster::fanny() naming character. Naming scheme clusters. One "asis" (output underlying cluster algorithm), "GOF" (ranks mean GOF values within clusters), "size" (ranks size clusters). Outliers detected dbscan hdbscan always named 0. warn logical. warning message least one cluster contains less (threshold * 100)% total paths? threshold numeric. significance threshold fraction total amount paths (0.01 default). one cluster contains less paths per total paths value, warning message created. Ignored warn FALSE. ... additional arguments passed cluster method.

title: Clustering thermal histories models — cluster_paths
text: data.frame path segment (integer) cluster (factor)

title: Clustering thermal histories models — cluster_paths
text: want use different clustering method built current version thermoclustr, can use distance matrix produced path_diss() feed cluster algorithm.

title: Clustering thermal histories models — cluster_paths
text: dbscan hdbscan methods require eps minPts arguments. Optimal eps values can visually estimated "knee" k-nearest neighbor distance plot using dbscan::kNNdistplot()'.

title: Homogenize path family names from several samples — cluster_samples
text: list. 'paths' sf object median cooling paths sample's cluster 'diss Dissimilarity matrix median paths 'hopkins' Hopkins statistic clustering 'dist' Used dissimilarity measure 'cluster' data.frame old new cluster assignments median paths (segments). 'mds' data.frame multidimensional scaling coordinates dissimilarity median paths 'cluster_method' clustering method used

title: Homogenize path family names from several samples — cluster_samples
text: default cluster algorithm assign comparable path families "hdbscan" trying find clusters least 2 sample clusters. algorithm may also find "outliers" (NA values cluster$cluster_new), clusters present samples.

title: Homogenize path family names from several samples — cluster_samples
code: # example data data(tT_paths) tT_paths_subset <- subset(tT_paths$paths, Comp_GOF >= 0.4) # calculate dissimilarity and find 3 clusters: tT_paths_subset_cl <- path_diss(tT_paths_subset) |> cluster_paths(3) tT_paths_subset_all <- merge(tT_paths_subset, tT_paths_subset_cl, by = "segment")  # create two random subsets of the same sample set.seed(20250411)  ## select 100 random path segments random_segments1 <- sample(unique(tT_paths$paths$segment), size = 100) tT_paths_rnd1 <- subset(tT_paths$paths, segment %in% random_segments1) tT_paths_rnd1_cl <- path_diss(tT_paths_rnd1) |>   cluster_paths(3) # calculate dissimilarity and find 3 clusters tT_paths_rnd1 <- merge(tT_paths_rnd1, tT_paths_rnd1_cl, by = "segment")  ## select 100 random path segments random_segments2 <- sample(unique(tT_paths$paths$segment), size = 100) tT_paths_rnd2 <- subset(tT_paths$paths, segment %in% random_segments2) tT_paths_rnd2_cl <- path_diss(tT_paths_rnd2) |>   cluster_paths(4) # calculate dissimilarity and find 4 clusters tT_paths_rnd2 <- merge(tT_paths_rnd2, tT_paths_rnd2_cl, by = "segment")  # combine all samples in a named list: sample_list <- list("all" = tT_paths_subset_all, "s1" = tT_paths_rnd1, "s2" = tT_paths_rnd2)  # check how the individial clusters compare to each other: cluster_samples(sample_list) #> $paths #> Simple feature collection with 10 features and 2 fields #> Geometry type: LINESTRING #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: 0 ymin: 23.24454 xmax: 40 ymax: 65.43562 #> CRS:           NA #> # A tibble: 10 × 3 #> 1.139026 25.46042, 1.95558 26.16914,… s1     1           #>  5 (0 23.24454, 0.9786171 24.58917, 1.931717 23.94287, 2.881… s1     2           #>  6 (0.1485138 27.78524, 1.18385 31.69537, 1.981107 31.4806, … s1     3           #>  7 (0.2105611 24.82631, 1.208798 26.05979, 2.006259 25.92004… s2     1           #>  8 (0.1854174 29.43847, 1.185072 36.67478, 1.999527 40.91297… s2     2           #>  9 (0 23.38913, 0.9953817 24.08435, 1.97012 24.231, 2.771527… s2     3           #> 10 (0 23.56792, 0.9552172 23.64332, 1.903507 23.90072, 2.861… s2     4           #>  #> $diss #>            1 14.945145 32.557487                     #> 7  13.220186  6.416828 12.973788  3.648220 19.633832 12.923656           #> 8  14.057358 19.955667  5.968636 19.346207 31.613409  6.125712 16.405143 #> 9  35.584225 24.489264 31.473238 20.481832  2.730208 35.287695 22.364039 #> 10 11.473066  7.799724 19.497439  9.574394 23.593637 19.362611 10.598462 #>            8         9 #> 2                      #> 3                      #> 4                      #> 5                      #> 6                      #> 7                      #> 8                      #> 9  34.343616           #> 10 22.056694 26.323751 #>  #> $hopkins #> statistic   p-value  #> 0.5545917 0.6467965  #>  #> $dist #> [1] "Hausdorff" #>  #> $mds #>             MDS1       MDS2 sample cluster_new cluster_old #> 1  -11.956042312  6.0997621    all           3           1 #> 2    0.165996586  6.3121044    all           2           2 #> 3   -9.105482180 -6.7360248    all           3           3 #> 4    2.744017829  2.4918257           1 #> 8  -11.572455541 -8.7502362     s2           3           2 #> 9   22.360112228 -3.7158714     s2           1           3 #> 10  -0.006714985 10.0382478     s2           2           4 #> what: Cluster tendency of thermochronology cooling paths — cluster_tendency
title: Cluster tendency of thermochronology cooling paths — cluster_tendency
text: Calculate Hopkins statistic Hausdorff Fréchet distance matrices check clusterability thermochronologic cooling paths. Calculated values 0-0.3 indicate regularly-spaced data. Values around 0.5 indicate random data. Values 0.7-1 indicate clustered data.

title: Cluster tendency of thermochronology cooling paths — cluster_tendency
Cluster tendency of thermochronology cooling paths — cluster_tendency
text: x either object class "tTdiss" (output path_diss()), distance matrix (object "dist"), data.frame containing coordinates dimension-reducing algorithm. m integer. Number rows sample x. NULL, rows considered. method character. Either "simple" (default) "torus".

title: Cluster tendency of thermochronology cooling paths — cluster_tendency
text: numeric. value Hopkins statistic range 0 1 p-value (null hypothesis spatial randomness).

title: Cluster tendency of thermochronology cooling paths — cluster_tendency
text: Calculates Hopkins statistic transformed Hausdorff Fréchet distance matrix using metric multidimensional scaling default.

title: Cluster tendency of thermochronology cooling paths — cluster_tendency
code: data(tT_paths) tT_paths_subset <- subset(tT_paths$paths, Comp_GOF >= 0.4)  # calculate the dissimilarities of the paths: tT_diss <- path_diss(tT_paths_subset, densify = 1)  set.seed(20250411) cluster_tendency(tT_diss) # H=0.77, p=2.88e-11 #>    statistic      p-value  #> 7.655771e-01 2.883072e-11   # Alternative dimension reducing algorithms can be used: # Kriskal's non-metric MDS library(MASS) tT_nmds <- isoMDS(tT_diss$diss)$points #> initial  value 11.693755  #> iter   5 value 9.168128 #> iter  10 value 8.906481 #> final  value 8.850736  #> converged cluster_tendency(tT_nmds) #>    statistic      p-value  #> 0.6601201750 0.0001442679  # Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP)   library(uwot) tT_umap <- umap2(tT_diss$diss) cluster_tendency(tT_umap) #> statistic   p-value  #>  0.908872  0.000000

what: Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths — path_diss
title: Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths — path_diss
text: Calculates dissimilarity matrix Hopkins statistic thermochronology cooling paths using Hausdorff Fréchet distance.

title: Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths — path_diss
code: path_diss(x, dist = c("Hausdorff", "Frechet"), densify = 0, simplify = 0, ...)  path_diss_parallel(x, simplify = 0, ...)

title: Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths — path_diss
text: x either object class "HeFTy" (output read_hefty()) data.frame containing time, temperature, segment columns modeled paths. dist character. Algorithm calculate dissimilarity matrix (distance) lines; either Hausdorff (default) Frechet. densify numeric. optionally use value 0 1 densify geometry description. Default 0. simplify numeric. Optional tolerance parameter specify amount simplification path geometries order save processing time. tolerance unit temperature relate expected temperature spread. simplification applied densification. good values 0.5, 2.5, 5, 10. Default 0 (simplification). ... (optional) arguments passed cluster_tendency().

title: Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths — path_diss
text: tTdiss object, .e. list containing paths t-T paths sf object time-temperature space diss \(n \times n\) dissimilarity matrix (\(n\) number paths) method dissimilarity algorithm used hopkins Hopkins statistic p-value. mds multidimensional scaling coordinates dissimilarity matrix

title: Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths — path_diss
text: Hausdorff distance greatest distances point one set closest point set. Fréchet distance additionally takes account location ordering points along curves ("flow").

title: Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths — path_diss
text: algorithm calculates pairwise dissimilarities n paths creates n x n matrix. large number paths may end long processing times can cause memory issues. Thus, recommended filter paths beforehand using either crop_paths() subset() , example, include paths large GOF (see examples), create random subset using, e.g., sample() (see examples). Additionally, simplify parameter can adjusted reduce number vertices along paths speed processing time.

title: Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths — path_diss

title: Hierarchical Clustering of Cooling Paths — path_hcut
Hierarchical Clustering of Cooling Paths — path_hcut
text: x object class "HeFTy", "tTdiss" "dist" (dissimilarity matrix). k integer. number clusters generated FUN hierarchical clustering function used, .e. one stats::hclust() (default), cluster::agnes(), cluster::diana()). ... optional arguments past hc_func

title: Hierarchical Clustering of Cooling Paths — path_hcut
text: object class "hcut" containing result standard function used (read documentation stats::hclust(), cluster::agnes(), cluster::diana()). includes also cluster cluster assignment observations cutting tree nbclust number clusters size size clusters

title: Hierarchical Clustering of Cooling Paths — path_hcut
code: data(tT_paths) tT_paths_subset <- subset(tT_paths$paths, Comp_GOF >= 0.4)  # calculate the dissimilarities of the paths: tT_diss <- path_diss(tT_paths_subset) path_hcut(tT_diss$diss, 3) #>  #> Call: #> FUN(d = x) #>  #> Cluster method   : complete  #> Number of objects: 68  #>

title: Optimal number of clusters — path_nbclust

title: Optimal number of clusters — path_nbclust

title: Optimal number of clusters — path_nbclust
text: x either object class "HeFTy" (output read_hefty()), "tTdiss (output path_diss()), data.frame containing time, temperature, segment columns modeled paths. x "tTdiss" object, calculated first path_diss() using default settings. FUNcluster cluster function. Default path_hcut(). k.max integer. maximum number clusters consider, must least two. n.threshold integer. number paths greater value, random sample size n.threshold paths selected dissimilarity matrix determine optimal number clusters. Must greater k.max. Default Inf, .e., sampling. Consider specify parameter processing takes much time. linecolor line color ... optionally arguments FUNcluster

title: Optimal number of clusters — path_nbclust
text: list. $optimal (integer) optimal number clusters, $plot ggplot showing average silhouette width number clusters.

title: Optimal number of clusters — path_nbclust
code: # example data data(tT_paths) set.seed(20250411) res1 <- path_nbclust(tT_paths, n.threshold = 100) res1 #> $optimal #> [1] 2 #>  #> $plot  #>   tT_paths_subset <- subset(tT_paths$paths, Comp_GOF >= 0.4) res2 <- path_nbclust(tT_paths_subset) res2 #> $optimal #> [1] 2 #>  #> $plot  #>

title: Plot the thermochronology paths — plot_paths
code: # example data data(tT_paths)  # Plot the paths: plot_paths(tT_paths)   # Show predefined path clusters: cl <- cluster_paths(tT_paths, 2) plot_paths(tT_paths, cluster = cl)   # Calculate cluster while plotting: plot_paths(tT_paths, do.cluster = TRUE, cluster.params = list(k = 3, method = "pam"))

title: Path Density Plot — plt_density
text: Creates 2d kernel density estimate t-T paths plots using ggplot

title: Path Density Plot — plt_density
code: plot_path_density_filled(   x,   bins = 50L,   densify = TRUE,   show.legend = NA,   geom = "density_2d_filled",   n = 100L,   ... )  plot_path_density(   x,   bins = 50L,   densify = TRUE,   show.legend = NA,   n = 100L,   ... )  plot_path_density_filled_weighted(   x,   bins = 50L,   densify = TRUE,   show.legend = NA,   n = 100L,   weights = NULL,   ... )

title: Path Density Plot — plt_density
text: x either object class "HeFTy" (output read_hefty()), data.frame containing time, temperature columns modeled paths, output densify_paths(). bins integer. Amount bins used kernel density estimate. 50 default. densify logical. Whether paths x densified first? Default TRUE. show.legend logical. layer included legends? NA, default, includes aesthetics mapped. FALSE never includes, TRUE always includes. can also named logical vector finely select aesthetics display. geom Use override default connection ggplot2::geom_density_2d() ggplot2::stat_density_2d(). information overriding connections, see stat geom arguments work. n integer. Number grid points direction. ... Arguments passed densify_paths() (densify=TRUE). weights numeric vector. Weights path segment.

title: Step ribbon statistic — stat_stepribbon

title: Step ribbon statistic — stat_stepribbon
text: copy stat_stepribbon function ggalt legacy package.

title: Step ribbon statistic — stat_stepribbon
text: mapping Set aesthetic mappings created aes(). specified inherit.aes = TRUE (default), combined default mapping top level plot. must supply mapping plot mapping. data data displayed layer. three options: NULL, default, data inherited plot data specified call ggplot(). data.frame, object, override plot data. objects fortified produce data frame. See fortify() variables created. function called single argument, plot data. return value must data.frame, used layer data. function can created formula (e.g. ~ head(.x, 10)). geom character. geom use; defaults "ribbon" position position adjustment use data layer. can used various ways, including prevent overplotting improving display. position argument accepts following: result calling position function, position_jitter(). method allows passing extra arguments position. string naming position adjustment. give position string, strip function name position_ prefix. example, use position_jitter(), give position "jitter". information ways specify position, see layer position documentation. na.rm FALSE, default, missing values removed warning. TRUE, missing values silently removed. show.legend logical. layer included legends? NA, default, includes aesthetics mapped. FALSE never includes, TRUE always includes. can also named logical vector finely select aesthetics display. include legend keys levels, even data exists, use TRUE.  NA, levels shown legend, unobserved levels omitted. inherit.aes FALSE, overrides default aesthetics, rather combining . useful helper functions define data aesthetics inherit behaviour default plot specification, e.g. annotation_borders(). direction character. "hv" horizontal-vertical steps, "vh" vertical-horizontal steps ... arguments passed layer()'s params argument. arguments broadly fall one 4 categories . Notably, arguments position argument, aesthetics required can passed .... Unknown arguments part 4 categories ignored. Static aesthetics mapped scale, fixed value apply layer whole. example, colour = "red" linewidth = 3. geom's documentation Aesthetics section lists available options. 'required' aesthetics passed params. Please note passing unmapped aesthetics vectors technically possible, order required length guaranteed parallel input data. constructing layer using stat_*() function, ... argument can used pass parameters geom part layer. example stat_density(geom = "area", outline.type = ""). geom's documentation lists parameters can accept. Inversely, constructing layer using geom_*() function, ... argument can used pass parameters stat part layer. example geom_area(stat = "density", adjust = 0.5). stat's documentation lists parameters can accept. key_glyph argument layer() may also passed .... can one functions described key glyphs, change display layer legend.

title: Step ribbon statistic — stat_stepribbon
text: https://groups.google.com/forum/?fromgroups=#!topic/ggplot2/9cFWHaH1CPs

title: Step ribbon statistic — stat_stepribbon
code: if (require("ggplot2")) {   x <- 1:10   df <- data.frame(x = x, y = x + 10, ymin = x + 7, ymax = x + 12)    ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +     geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ymin, ymax = ymax),       stat = "stepribbon", fill = "grey",       direction = "hv"     ) +     geom_step(color = "red")    ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +     geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ymin, ymax = ymax),       stat = "stepribbon", fill = "grey",       direction = "vh"     ) +     geom_step(color = "blue") } #> Loading required package: ggplot2
